title: Chapter 8: The First Offer
content: Dawn of day three.
Roxanne was woken by the sound of a chainsaw scraping against her door frame.
She sat up in the dean's leather chair, still in yesterday's clothes, neck stiff from sleeping upright. The Executioner stood in the doorway, one bandaged arm raised, its rusted mask fixed on her.
It pointed toward the main gate.
"Visitors?"
A single nod.
She grabbed her jacket. The black card was already in her pocket — she'd slept with it, unconsciously protective. The card was warm against her thigh, like a living thing.
Silas was in the lobby when she came down. He was at the coffee station, holding a cup. The foam art was perfect — a leaf pattern, precise and symmetrical.
"You have guests," he said without looking up.
"I heard."
"Commander Hayes. Three vehicles. Six armed personnel. She looks tired."
"You can sense all that from here?"
"I can sense a fly landing on a window three blocks away. A convoy of vehicles is not subtle."
He handed her the coffee. She took a sip. It was the best latte she'd ever had — perfectly balanced, the oat milk steamed to exactly the right temperature.
"You learned to do this in one day?"
"I learned to forge galaxies in thirty minutes. Latte art was not difficult."
"That's terrifying."
"Thank you."
She walked out to meet the Coalition.
The morning was cold. The violet sky had lightened to a bruised lavender, shot through with veins of deeper purple. The air smelled like ozone and wet stone.
Hayes stood at the main gate, alone this time. Her six personnel waited by the vehicles, fifty feet back, hands on their weapons but not raised.
"Commander. You're back early."
"Voss wants to meet you."
"Colonel Voss. The man who runs the Salvation Coalition."
"The same." Hayes's jaw was tight. "He heard about the campus. Heard about the card. He wants to discuss a partnership."
The way she said "partnership" suggested it wasn't her word.
"A partnership," Roxanne repeated. "That's a polite way of saying he wants a piece of the action."
"I'm not going to lie to you. That's exactly what it is. But it's also not the worst option you have. The Coalition has resources. Territory. People. You have..."
Hayes gestured vaguely at Roxanne, at the campus behind her, at the Executioner lurking in the gatehouse, at Silas standing in the lobby doorway with his shadow-coat rippling.
"...whatever all of this is."
"And if I say no?"
Hayes was quiet for a long moment.
"Voss will try to take it. Not today. Probably not tomorrow. But he'll try. He's a collector. He sees something valuable and he needs to own it."
"Then tell Voss I'll meet him. Neutral ground. The public library on Fifth and Main — it's still standing. Tomorrow. Noon."
Hayes nodded. "I'll relay the message."
She turned to leave, then stopped.
"Roxanne."
"Yes?"
"Be careful. Voss isn't stupid. But he's desperate. And desperate men do stupid things."
"I'll keep that in mind."
Hayes walked back to the convoy. The vehicles pulled away, their engines echoing off the empty buildings.
Silas appeared beside Roxanne. She hadn't heard him approach.
"She's right," he said quietly. "The man is afraid. I could smell it on her — borrowed fear. She's afraid FOR him, which means she knows something he doesn't want to admit."
"What's he afraid of?"
"You. The card. The fact that his entire empire just became obsolete."
Roxanne finished her coffee.
"Good," she said. "Let him be afraid."
*End of Chapter 8*
Back in the dean's office, Roxanne spread out a campus map and started planning.
"If Voss wants to negotiate, I need leverage. What do I have that he wants?"
"The card itself," Silas said. "Protection. The ability to purchase Nether-Grid resources without incurring debt."
"And what does he have that I want?"
"Information. Manpower. The hospital. The water treatment plant. He controls infrastructure you can't replicate overnight."
"So it's a trade. Not a takeover."
"If you handle it correctly."
Roxanne looked at the map. The Coalition controlled about sixty percent of the downtown area. She controlled one university campus and its underground ley node. The disparity was significant.
But the card changed the equation.
"I need to see what else I can buy," she said. "Before the meeting. I need to know what my options are."
She held up the card.
*DISPLAY: AVAILABLE PROPERTIES — EASTERN DISTRICT.*
The translucent screen appeared. A list of every building, infrastructure asset, and territory within a five-mile radius, each with a price tag.

The hospital: 18 million Obols.
The water treatment plant: 8 million.
The power substation: 4.5 million.
The police station: 3 million.
The library (neutral ground for tomorrow): 500,000 Obols.
"I could buy the entire district," she murmured. "Everything Voss controls. Out from under him."
"You could," Silas agreed. "But that would make him your enemy permanently. Negotiation turns competitors into partners. Conquest turns competitors into insurgents."
Roxanne looked at him.
"You give surprisingly good advice for a god who was sealed for four billion years."
"I've had time to think about strategy. When you can't move, you learn to think."
She studied the map for another long moment.
Then she smiled.
"Okay. I won't buy the district. But I'm going to buy the library tonight. Before the meeting."
"Why?"

"Because tomorrow, when I walk into that negotiation, I want to walk into a building I own."
\n---\n
Commander Hayes was already waiting at the library, standing in the shadow of the front portico. She looked smaller than Roxanne remembered. The apocalypse was wearing on her.
"Colonel Voss agreed to neutral ground," she said as Roxanne approached. "But he's bringing his own security."
"So am I."

Hayes looked at Silas. Then at the Executioner, who had positioned itself on the library's roof, chainsaw gleaming faintly. Then at two figures in the distance — the Enforcement units Roxanne had purchased, standing motionless at the corners.
"That's... more security than I expected."
"I'm an over-preparer."
"You're building an army."
"I'm building a safety net. There's a difference."
Hayes didn't argue. She'd seen what Roxanne could do. She knew arguing was pointless.
"He'll be here in ten minutes."
"Good. I want to get a coffee before he arrives."

She walked into the library. The interior was grand — marble floors, oak shelves, a reading room with a fireplace that was somehow still functioning. She sat at the main table, card in front of her, and waited.
Roxanne walked back to the Fine Arts building, the latte warm in her hands. The campus was quiet. Too quiet. The apocalypse had a way of silencing things.
She found Lena in the studio, sketching a series of quick portraits. Survivors. Their faces, their fear, the way their hands held whatever they'd managed to save.
"You're documenting everyone?"
"Someone should. If we survive this, people will want to know what it looked like. What it felt like."
Roxanne sat on the stool beside her. "The Coalition wants to meet. Tomorrow. Neutral ground."
"And you're going?"
"I'm going."
"Alone?"
"With Silas. And the Executioner. And the two Enforcement units I bought."
Lena's pencil paused. "That's six beings, two of which are cosmic horrors."
"Five. One of the Enforcement units has to stay and guard the gate."
"That's still a lot of firepower for a meeting."
"The apocalypse has taught me that there's no such thing as too much firepower."
The pencil resumed its work.
"Be careful, Roxy."
"I'm always careful."
"You bought a god yesterday."
"I was very careful about it. I looked at the price twice."
